subject: Renal System
front: What is renal clearance?
back: The volume of plasma/blood that is completely cleared of a substance by the kidneys per unit time.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the formula for clearance?
back: Clearance = (Urine concentration ÷ Plasma concentration) × Urine flow rate (ml/min); i.e. (U×V)/P

subject: Renal System
front: Which substance's clearance is used as a proxy for GFR and why?
back: Inulin — it is freely filtered, neither reabsorbed nor secreted, so its clearance equals GFR (passes unchanged).

subject: Renal System
front: Which substance's clearance is used as a proxy for RPF and why?
back: PAH (para-aminohippuric acid) — it is filtered AND secreted, so almost all of it is cleared in a single pass, making its clearance equal to RPF.

subject: Renal System
front: How is Renal Blood Flow (RBF) calculated from RPF?
back: RBF = RPF / (1 − Hematocrit)

subject: Renal System
front: What is the filtration fraction and its normal value?
back: Filtration fraction = GFR/RPF = Inulin clearance/PAH clearance ≈ 20%

subject: Renal System
front: Rank PAH, creatinine, inulin, urea, Na, and glucose by clearance (highest to lowest).
back: PAH > Creatinine > Inulin > Urea > Na > Glucose

subject: Renal System
front: Why is the clearance of PAH and creatinine greater than GFR (i.e. greater than inulin clearance)?
back: Because they undergo additional tubular secretion on top of filtration.

subject: Renal System
front: Why is the clearance of glucose, sodium, and bicarbonate less than GFR?
back: Because they are reabsorbed by the renal tubules after filtration.

subject: Renal System
front: On a graph of % filtered load remaining vs nephron length, how does PAH behave?
back: It rises above 100% and plateaus near/above 100%, because it is added to the tubular fluid by secretion.

subject: Renal System
front: On a graph of % filtered load remaining vs nephron length, how does inulin behave?
back: It stays flat at 100% throughout the nephron since it is freely filtered but neither reabsorbed nor secreted.

subject: Renal System
front: On a graph of % filtered load remaining vs nephron length, how do HCO3-/Na and glucose behave?
back: Both decline toward 0% due to reabsorption; glucose reaches zero earliest (fully reabsorbed), followed later by HCO3-/Na.

subject: Renal System
front: On a Tubular fluid/Plasma (TF/P) ratio graph along the PCT, which solutes rise highest and which fall to zero?
back: PAH and creatinine rise highest (secreted); urea/Cl-/K+ rise mildly; osmolarity/Na+ stay near 1; HCO3-, amino acids, and glucose fall toward 0 (reabsorbed).

subject: Renal System
front: What structure is the counter-current multiplier and what is its role?
back: The Loop of Henle — it establishes the corticomedullary counter-current osmotic gradient.

subject: Renal System
front: What structure is the counter-current exchanger and what is its role?
back: The Vasa recta — it maintains the counter-current gradient without dissipating it.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the three components of the juxtaglomerular apparatus?
back: Macula densa, JG (granular) cells, and Lacis (extraglomerular mesangial) cells.

subject: Renal System
front: Describe the macula densa: location, receptor type, and feedback loop.
back: Located between the DCT and thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle; acts as a chemoreceptor; mediates tubulo-glomerular feedback.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens when the macula densa senses increased Na+ delivery?
back: It releases adenosine, which causes afferent arteriole vasoconstriction and decreases GFR.

subject: Renal System
front: Describe JG cells: location, receptor type, and function.
back: Modified smooth muscle cells of the afferent arteriole; act as baroreceptors; mediate glomerulo-tubular feedback; activate RAAS when GFR/BP is low.

subject: Renal System
front: What stimulates JG cells to release renin?
back: A drop in blood pressure/GFR sensed via the baroreceptor mechanism, and sympathetic β1 stimulation.

subject: Renal System
front: What are Lacis cells and what is their function?
back: Mesangial cells of the JG apparatus that have an anti-inflammatory function.

subject: Renal System
front: Outline the RAAS cascade from angiotensinogen to angiotensin II.
back: Angiotensinogen (from liver) → via Renin (from JG cells) → Angiotensin I → via ACE (from lung endothelium) → Angiotensin II.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the vascular actions of Angiotensin II?
back: Potent vasoconstrictor, with preferential constriction of the efferent arteriole.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the renal (PCT) actions of Angiotensin II?
back: Increases Na+, H2O, and HCO3- reabsorption, producing a 'contraction alkalosis.'

subject: Renal System
front: What are the hormonal actions of Angiotensin II?
back: Stimulates aldosterone release, stimulates ADH release, and stimulates thirst.

subject: Renal System
front: Where is aldosterone produced and what does it do at the collecting duct?
back: Produced in the zona glomerulosa; acts on principal cells to increase Na+ and water reabsorption and increase K+ and H+ excretion.

subject: Renal System
front: Besides converting Angiotensin I to II, what else does ACE do, and why is this clinically important?
back: ACE also breaks down bradykinin; this explains why ACE inhibitors can cause cough and angioedema due to bradykinin accumulation.

subject: Renal System
front: List the 3 ADH (vasopressin) receptors with their locations and actions.
back: V1 — vascular smooth muscle, causes vasoconstriction. V2 — collecting duct principal cells, mediates water reabsorption (also causes release of vWF and Factor VIII). V3 — anterior pituitary, stimulates ACTH release.

subject: Renal System
front: Over what MAP range are renal blood flow and GFR autoregulated?
back: MAP of 80–180 mmHg.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the two mechanisms of renal autoregulation?
back: (1) Myogenic mechanism, (2) Tubuloglomerular feedback via the macula densa.

subject: Renal System
front: Define the 'splay' phenomenon in renal glucose handling.
back: The early appearance of glucose in urine before the theoretical transport maximum (Tm) is reached.

subject: Renal System
front: What causes the splay phenomenon?
back: Nephron-to-nephron variability — not all SGLT transporters have identical capacity, so some nephrons reach saturation earlier than others.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the approximate Tm for glucose and the renal threshold for glucosuria?
back: Tm ≈ 375 mg/min; renal threshold ≈ 200 mg/dl plasma glucose.

subject: Renal System
front: What does cystometrography (CMG) measure?
back: Intravesical pressure versus bladder volume during bladder filling.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens to bladder pressure during the filling phase, and why?
back: A large increase in volume occurs with only a very small rise in intravesical pressure, due to high bladder compliance — explained by Laplace's Law.

subject: Renal System
front: State Laplace's Law as applied to the bladder.
back: P = 2T/R (Pressure = 2 × wall tension ÷ radius).

subject: Renal System
front: Approximately what bladder volume marks the transition from filling to voiding phase on a cystometrogram?
back: Around 400 ml.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the parasympathetic ('Pee') actions on the bladder during voiding?
back: Detrusor muscle contracts; internal urethral sphincter relaxes.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the sympathetic ('Store') actions on the bladder during storage?
back: Detrusor relaxes; internal urethral sphincter contracts.

subject: Renal System
front: Which nerve provides somatic (voluntary) control of micturition, and what does it control?
back: The pudendal nerve (S2–S4); it controls the external urethral sphincter.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the formula for Net Filtration Pressure (NFP) and its components?
back: NFP = (PGC + πBS) − (PBS + πGC); PGC = glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure, πBS = Bowman's capsule oncotic pressure (≈0), PBS = Bowman's capsule hydrostatic pressure, πGC = glomerular capillary oncotic pressure.

subject: Renal System
front: Using classic values PGC=45 mmHg, πBS=0, PBS=10 mmHg, πGC=25 mmHg, what is the NFP?

subject: Renal System
front: Effect of afferent arteriole constriction on GFR, RPF, and FF?
back: GFR↓, RPF↓, FF unchanged.

subject: Renal System
front: Effect of efferent arteriole constriction on GFR, RPF, and FF?
back: GFR↑, RPF↓, FF↑↑ (markedly increased).

subject: Renal System
front: Effect of high plasma protein concentration on GFR, RPF, and FF?
back: GFR↓, RPF unchanged, FF↓.

subject: Renal System
front: Effect of low plasma protein concentration on GFR, RPF, and FF?
back: GFR↑, RPF unchanged, FF↑.

subject: Renal System
front: Effect of ureter constriction on GFR, RPF, and FF?
back: GFR↓, RPF unchanged, FF↓.

subject: Renal System
front: Effect of dehydration on GFR, RPF, and FF?
back: GFR↓, RPF↓↓ (markedly decreased), FF↑ (increased).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the three layers of the glomerular filtration barrier?
back: (1) Fenestrated capillary endothelium, (2) Glomerular basement membrane (GBM), (3) Podocyte foot processes (visceral epithelial layer).

subject: Renal System
front: Describe the fenestrated capillary endothelium layer of the filtration barrier.
back: Pore size 50–100 nm; prevents passage of cellular elements (RBCs, platelets).

subject: Renal System
front: Describe the GBM layer of the filtration barrier.
back: Contains type IV collagen and heparan sulfate (negatively charged); provides size and charge selectivity; is the major barrier to protein filtration.

subject: Renal System
front: Describe the podocyte foot process layer of the filtration barrier.
back: Forms the slit diaphragm; slit pore size ~4 nm.

subject: Renal System
front: What mnemonic describes prostaglandins' effect on the afferent arteriole, and the consequence of NSAIDs?
back: "PDA" – Prostaglandins Dilate the Afferent arteriole; NSAIDs (prostaglandin inhibitors) cause afferent constriction and reduce GFR.

subject: Renal System
front: What mnemonic describes Angiotensin II's effect on the efferent arteriole?
back: "ACE" – Angiotensin Constricts the Efferent arteriole.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the effect of ACE inhibitors/ARBs on the efferent arteriole, and in what condition are they contraindicated?
back: They dilate the efferent arteriole; contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis (can precipitate AKI by dropping GFR further).

subject: Renal System
front: Why are ACEi/ARBs considered 'reno-protective' and preferred as DOC in diabetes and CKD?
back: Efferent arteriole dilation reduces intraglomerular pressure, slowing progression of diabetic nephropathy/CKD.

subject: Renal System
front: Why should NSAIDs and ACEi/ARB never be combined?
back: Combining afferent constriction (NSAIDs) with efferent dilation (ACEi/ARB) can critically drop GFR and precipitate AKI.

subject: Renal System
front: Where is ANP released from, what stimulates its release, and what is its renal effect?
back: Released from the atria in response to volume expansion; causes afferent arteriole dilatation, increases GFR, and promotes sodium and water excretion.

subject: Renal System
front: What mnemonic lists the substances that increase GFR via mesangial relaxation, and what are they?
back: "DANCE2" – Dopamine, ANP, Nitric oxide (NO), cAMP, PGE2.

subject: Renal System
front: How does relative filterability change with increasing effective molecular radius?
back: Filterability decreases as molecular radius increases (true for cation, neutral, and anion curves alike).

subject: Renal System
front: For a given molecular radius, how does filterability differ between cations, neutral molecules, and anions, and why?
back: Cations > neutral > anions, because the negatively charged GBM (heparan sulfate) repels anions and is relatively more permeable to cations.

subject: Renal System
front: In circulatory shock, what happens to afferent and efferent arterioles, and to RPF/GFR/FF?
back: Both afferent and efferent arterioles are constricted, causing low RPF and low GFR, while filtration fraction is increased.

subject: Renal System
front: What structural features characterize the PCT?
back: An extensive brush border (increases surface area for reabsorption) and leaky tight junctions (allow paracellular transport).

subject: Renal System
front: What is the PCT's overall role in nephron reabsorption?
back: It is the major site of reabsorption in the nephron — all electrolytes are maximally reabsorbed here except Mg2+.

subject: Renal System
front: What percentage of filtered water is reabsorbed in the PCT, and is this iso-osmotic or aniso-osmotic?
back: About 60% of filtered water is reabsorbed in the PCT, isotonically (isosmotic reabsorption), along with glucose, HCO3-, urea, and amino acids.

subject: Renal System
front: Which glucose transporters mediate PCT glucose reabsorption, and what proportion does each handle?
back: SGLT2 reabsorbs 90–95% of glucose in the early PCT; SGLT1 reabsorbs the remaining 5–10% in the late proximal straight tubule.

subject: Renal System
front: What proportion of filtered HCO3- is reabsorbed in the PCT and via which enzyme?
back: 80–90% of filtered HCO3- is reabsorbed in the PCT using carbonic anhydrase.

subject: Renal System
front: What effect does HCO3- reabsorption have on urine pH in the PCT, and how is this reabsorption regulated?
back: It relatively acidifies the urine; reabsorption is increased by Angiotensin II (contributing to contraction alkalosis) and decreased by Acetazolamide and type 2 RTA.

subject: Renal System
front: Where do SGLT-2 inhibitors act?
back: On the apical membrane of the PCT, blocking Na+/glucose cotransport.

subject: Renal System
front: Which apical PCT exchanger does Angiotensin II stimulate?
back: The Na+/H+ exchanger.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the effects of PTH on the renal tubules?
back: PTH inhibits phosphate reabsorption in the PCT and increases Ca2+ reabsorption in the DCT.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the key characteristics of the thin descending limb of the loop of Henle?
back: Permeable only to water (impermeable to solutes); contains Aquaporin 1.

subject: Renal System
front: What is a key functional feature of the TAL regarding water permeability, and its consequence?
back: It is impermeable to water — this is where the urine begins to dilute.

subject: Renal System
front: What transporter mediates TAL solute reabsorption, and how much of the filtered load does it reabsorb?
back: The Na+/K+/2Cl- (NKCC2) cotransporter on the luminal membrane; reabsorbs about 10–15% of filtered Na+, K+, and Cl-.

subject: Renal System
front: What blocks the TAL Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter pharmacologically, and what genetic disease mimics this?
back: Loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide); Bartter syndrome (mnemonic: 'Bartter in the loop').

subject: Renal System
front: What is the role of ROMK channels in the TAL?
back: They recycle K+ back into the tubular lumen, creating a luminal positive potential that drives paracellular reabsorption of Ca2+ and Mg2+.

subject: Renal System
front: What is a key functional feature of the DCT regarding water permeability?
back: It is relatively impermeable to water, so urine dilution continues here.

subject: Renal System
front: What transporter mediates DCT NaCl reabsorption, and how much does it reabsorb?
back: The Na+-Cl- (NCC) cotransporter on the luminal membrane; reabsorbs about 5–10% of filtered NaCl.

subject: Renal System
front: What blocks the DCT Na+-Cl- cotransporter pharmacologically, and what genetic disease mimics this?
back: Thiazide diuretics; Gitelman syndrome (mnemonic: 'Gitelman for thigh'/DCT).

subject: Renal System
front: How does the DCT handle calcium reabsorption?
back: PTH increases Ca2+ reabsorption in the DCT.

subject: Renal System
front: How does the DCT handle magnesium reabsorption, and what disrupts it?
back: Via TRPM6 channels; blocked by thiazide diuretics or in Gitelman syndrome, causing hypomagnesemia.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the two main transport functions of collecting duct principal cells?
back: Na+ and water reabsorption, and K+ secretion.

subject: Renal System
front: How does ADH regulate water reabsorption in principal cells?
back: Via V2 receptors, ADH inserts aquaporin-2 channels into the luminal membrane, allowing free water reabsorption.

subject: Renal System
front: How much water is reabsorbed in the collecting duct with and without ADH?
back: 1–2% water reabsorption without ADH; about 10% water reabsorption with ADH.

subject: Renal System
front: What channel mediates Na+ reabsorption in principal cells, and what drugs block it?
back: ENaC (epithelial Na+ channel); blocked by amiloride and triamterene.

subject: Renal System
front: What disease results from a gain-of-function mutation in ENaC?
back: Liddle's syndrome.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the effects of aldosterone on collecting duct principal cells?
back: Increases Na+ and water reabsorption, and increases K+ and H+ secretion.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the function of alpha (α) intercalated cells, and what disorder results from their dysfunction?
back: They secrete H+ (important for correcting acidosis); reduced H+ secretion causes Type 1 RTA.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the function of beta (β) intercalated cells?
back: They secrete HCO3- (important for correcting alkalosis).

subject: Renal System
front: What is the mechanism underlying Type 4 RTA?
back: Aldosterone deficiency/resistance, leading to reduced Na+ reabsorption and reduced K+/H+ secretion in the collecting duct.

subject: Renal System
front: What is unique about the medullary collecting duct, and why is this important?
back: It reabsorbs urea, which helps maintain the corticomedullary osmotic gradient essential for concentrating urine.

subject: Renal System
front: Without ADH, what is the minimum tubular fluid osmolality reached, and where?
back: 50 mOsm/kg, in the cortical collecting duct.

subject: Renal System
front: Without ADH, what is the maximum tubular fluid osmolality reached, and where?
back: 600 mOsm/kg, at the tip of the loop of Henle.

subject: Renal System
front: With ADH, what is the minimum tubular fluid osmolality reached, and where?
back: 300 mOsm/kg, in the DCT.

subject: Renal System
front: With ADH, what is the maximum tubular fluid osmolality reached, and where (as labeled in the source)?
back: 1200 mOsm/kg, in the collecting duct (source labels this the 'cortical collecting duct,' though maximal concentration of 1200 mOsm/kg is achieved as fluid passes through the medullary/papillary portion of the collecting duct).

subject: Renal System
front: Where in the nephron does the overall maximum water (H2O) reabsorption occur?
back: The proximal convoluted tubule (PCT).

subject: Renal System
front: Why does collecting duct fluid become progressively more dilute (down to 50 mOsm/kg) in the absence of ADH?
back: Without ADH the collecting duct is impermeable to water, so as fluid passes through the hypertonic medulla, solute (NaCl) is reabsorbed without water, progressively diluting the fluid.

subject: Renal System
front: What role does urea play in the 'with ADH' state of urine concentration?
back: Urea reabsorbed from the medullary collecting duct (facilitated by ADH) recycles into the medullary interstitium, contributing to the high medullary osmolality (up to 1200 mOsm/kg) needed to concentrate urine.

subject: Renal System
front: Where and how does acetazolamide act?
back: Blocks carbonic anhydrase in the PCT.

subject: Renal System
front: What type of RTA does acetazolamide cause, and what mnemonic helps remember this?
back: Type 2 (proximal) RTA — mnemonic 'Acida-zolamide' (HCO3- is lost in urine).

subject: Renal System
front: Why can acetazolamide worsen hepatic encephalopathy?
back: It impairs ammonia excretion, causing NH3 toxicity, which worsens hepatic encephalopathy.

subject: Renal System
front: Why does acetazolamide cause hypokalemia?
back: Sodium not absorbed in the PCT is delivered to the distal tubule where it promotes K+ loss via ROMK channels.

subject: Renal System
front: List other adverse effects of acetazolamide.
back: Paresthesia (tingling), calcium phosphate kidney stones (due to alkaline urine), and sulfa allergy.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the clinical uses of acetazolamide?
back: Altitude (mountain) sickness, and familial hypokalemic periodic paralysis.

subject: Renal System
front: Where and how do thiazide-like diuretics act?
back: They block the Na+-Cl- cotransporter in the DCT.

subject: Renal System
front: What acid-base and sodium disturbances do thiazides cause?
back: Metabolic alkalosis and hyponatremia (due to sodium loss in the distal tubule itself).

subject: Renal System
front: How do thiazides affect calcium handling, and what conditions does this make them useful for?
back: They increase calcium reabsorption (decrease urine calcium) — preferred in osteoporosis and in nephrolithiasis (reduces calcium stone formation).

subject: Renal System
front: What metabolic side effects (besides sulfa allergy) are caused by thiazides?
back: Hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, and hyperlipidemia.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the clinical uses of thiazide-like diuretics?
back: Hypertension, heart failure, idiopathic hypercalciuria, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and osteoporosis.

subject: Renal System
front: Which thiazide-like diuretic is longest-acting, and what is a distinguishing effect?
back: Chlorthalidone — longest acting; increases serum calcium.

subject: Renal System
front: Which thiazide is shortest-acting and available as a 'true' IV thiazide?
back: Chlorthiazide.

subject: Renal System
front: Which thiazide-like diuretic has a vasodilator effect and undergoes hepatic excretion?
back: Indapamide.

subject: Renal System
front: Which thiazide-like diuretic is safest in renal failure, and at what threshold?
back: Metolazone — safe even at GFR < 30 ml/min per coreBTR (note: BTR 2.0 handwritten annotation cites an eGFR cutoff of <40 — sources differ slightly, flag for verification).

subject: Renal System
front: Where and how do loop diuretics act?
back: They block the Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter in the thick ascending limb (TAL) of the loop of Henle.

subject: Renal System
front: Why are loop diuretics called 'high ceiling' diuretics, and what is their classic use?
back: They produce a much greater diuresis than other classes; classically used in cardiogenic pulmonary edema.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens to urinary calcium with loop diuretics, and what is the consequence?
back: Loop diuretics cause calcium loss in urine ('loops lose calcium'), predisposing to urinary calcium stones.

subject: Renal System
front: What electrolyte/acid-base disturbance results from increased distal Na+ delivery with loop diuretics?
back: Hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis.

subject: Renal System
front: List the side effects of loop diuretics.
back: Ototoxicity, hyperuricemia, sulfa allergy, and interstitial nephritis.

subject: Renal System
front: Name the loop diuretics and their key distinguishing features.
back: Furosemide (lasix) — most commonly used; Torsemide — longest acting with good oral bioavailability; Bumetanide — most potent; Ethacrynic acid — not a sulfa drug (safe in sulfa allergy) but carries the highest risk of ototoxicity.

subject: Renal System
front: What class of diuretic is mannitol, and what are its clinical uses?
back: Osmotic diuretic; used in acute angle-closure glaucoma, cerebral edema, and dialysis disequilibrium syndrome.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the side effects of mannitol?
back: Dehydration; hyponatremia (from fluid overload) or hypernatremia (from diuresis); pulmonary edema.

subject: Renal System
front: Why is mannitol contraindicated in anuria?
back: Because it stays in the blood (cannot be excreted), causing fluid overload.

subject: Renal System
front: Why is mannitol contraindicated in heart failure?
back: It initially increases blood volume, which can precipitate pulmonary edema.

subject: Renal System
front: What mnemonic covers the K+-sparing diuretics, and what are they?
back: 'SEAT' — Spironolactone, Eplerenone, Amiloride, Triamterene.

subject: Renal System
front: How do spironolactone/eplerenone work, and what are spironolactone's notable side effects?
back: They are aldosterone (mineralocorticoid receptor) antagonists (MRAs); spironolactone causes gynecomastia, antiandrogen effects, and metabolic acidosis.

subject: Renal System
front: How do amiloride/triamterene work?
back: They block ENaC (epithelial Na+ channel).

subject: Renal System
front: What are MRAs (spironolactone/eplerenone) the drug of choice for?
back: Ascites and resistant hypertension.

subject: Renal System
front: What is amiloride the drug of choice for?
back: Lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, Liddle syndrome, and cystic fibrosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What is unique about finerenone among MRAs?
back: It is the only non-steroidal MRA, with equal bioavailability in the heart and kidney.

subject: Renal System
front: What is lorundrostat and its use?
back: An aldosterone synthase inhibitor (ASI) used for resistant hypertension.

subject: Renal System
front: Rank hyperuricemia risk between thiazides and loop diuretics.
back: Thiazide > Loop.

subject: Renal System
front: Which diuretic classes carry a risk of sulfa allergy?
back: Acetazolamide, loop diuretics, and thiazides.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the underlying defect and location in Type 2 RTA?
back: Decreased HCO3- reabsorption in the proximal tubule.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the underlying defect and location in Type 1 RTA?
back: Decreased H+ secretion in the distal tubule — the most severe type.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the underlying defect and location in Type 4 RTA?
back: Hypoaldosteronism affecting the collecting tubule.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the serum K+, serum Ca2+, urinary pH, and nephrolithiasis findings in Type 2 RTA?
back: K+↓, Ca2+↓, urinary pH↓ (acidic urine), nephrolithiasis absent.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the serum K+, serum Ca2+, urinary pH, and nephrolithiasis findings in Type 1 RTA?
back: K+↓, Ca2+↑, urinary pH↑ (alkaline urine), nephrolithiasis strongly present — alkaline urine plus hypercalciuria predispose to stones/nephrocalcinosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the serum K+, serum Ca2+, urinary pH, and nephrolithiasis findings in Type 4 RTA?
back: K+↑ (hyperkalemia — key distinguishing feature), Ca2+↓, urinary pH↓ (acidic urine), nephrolithiasis absent.

subject: Renal System
front: What diseases classically cause Type 2 (proximal) RTA as part of Fanconi syndrome?
back: Fanconi's disease, Wilson's disease, Lowe syndrome, and cystinosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What is Fanconi syndrome?
back: Generalized proximal tubular dysfunction causing impaired reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, phosphate, and bicarbonate — overlaps with Type 2 RTA.

subject: Renal System
front: What drugs cause Type 2 RTA?
back: Ifosfamide, cisplatin, and tenofovir.

subject: Renal System
front: What other (non-drug, non-classic-disease) causes of Type 2 RTA exist?
back: Lead poisoning and multiple myeloma.

subject: Renal System
front: What drug is classically associated with Type 1 (distal) RTA?
back: Amphotericin B.

subject: Renal System
front: What autoimmune diseases cause Type 1 (distal) RTA?
back: Sjögren's syndrome, SLE, and rheumatoid arthritis.

subject: Renal System
front: What causes Type 4 RTA?
back: Conditions causing hypoaldosteronism, such as obstructive uropathy and heparin use.

subject: Renal System
front: What transporter defect underlies Bartter syndrome, and what diuretic does it mimic?
back: Loss of function of the Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter in the TAL — mimics loop diuretics ('Bartter in the loop').

subject: Renal System
front: What are the BP, K+, acid-base, and calcium findings in Bartter syndrome?
back: BP↓, K+↓, metabolic alkalosis, serum Ca2+↓ with urine Ca2+↑.

subject: Renal System
front: What transporter defect underlies Gitelman syndrome, and what diuretic does it mimic?
back: Loss of function of the Na+-Cl- cotransporter in the DCT — mimics thiazide diuretics ('Gitelman for thigh'/DCT).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the BP, K+, acid-base, and calcium findings in Gitelman syndrome?
back: BP↓, K+↓, metabolic alkalosis, serum Ca2+↑ with urine Ca2+↓, plus hypomagnesemia.

subject: Renal System
front: What defect underlies Gordon syndrome, and what are its BP/K+/pH findings?
back: Gain-of-function of the DCT Na-Cl cotransporter; causes BP↑, K+↑, metabolic acidosis, with no calcium effect.

subject: Renal System
front: What defect underlies pseudohypoaldosteronism, and what are its BP/K+/pH findings?
back: Functional inhibition of collecting duct ENaC (aldosterone resistance); causes BP↓, K+↑, metabolic acidosis, with no calcium effect.

subject: Renal System
front: What defect underlies Liddle syndrome, and what are its BP/K+/pH findings?
back: Gain-of-function mutation of ENaC in the collecting duct; causes BP↑, K+↓, metabolic alkalosis, with no calcium effect.

subject: Renal System
front: Why does hypokalemia lead to metabolic alkalosis in disorders like Bartter/Gitelman/Liddle syndrome?
back: When K+ is low, the distal nephron minimizes further K+ loss by having the collecting duct reabsorb Na+ with increased H+ secretion instead of K+ secretion, causing increased urinary H+ loss and metabolic alkalosis.

subject: Renal System
front: List the differential diagnoses for hypertension + hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis.
back: (1) Primary hyperaldosteronism from an adrenal adenoma (Conn syndrome), (2) a renin-producing tumor, (3) Liddle syndrome, (4) Syndrome of Apparent Mineralocorticoid Excess (SAME) due to 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase (11β-HSD) deficiency.

subject: Renal System
front: What disease(s) are associated with subepithelial immune deposits, and what are their characteristic appearances?
back: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) — 'humps'; Membranous nephropathy — 'spike and dome' pattern.

subject: Renal System
front: What disease is associated with intramembranous deposits?
back: MPGN type 2 (dense deposit disease).

subject: Renal System
front: What disease(s) are associated with subendothelial deposits?
back: MPGN type 1 and SLE (lupus) nephritis.

subject: Renal System
front: What disease(s) are associated with mesangial deposits?
back: IgA nephropathy (Berger's disease) and RPGN.

subject: Renal System
front: Which type IV collagen alpha chain is targeted in Goodpasture syndrome, and what is the immunofluorescence pattern?
back: The α3 chain; linear immunofluorescence pattern (anti-GBM antibodies).

subject: Renal System
front: Which type IV collagen alpha chain is affected in thin basement membrane disease, and what is its alternate name?
back: The α4 chain; also known as benign familial hematuria.

subject: Renal System
front: Which type IV collagen alpha chain is affected in Alport syndrome, and what is its inheritance pattern?
back: The α5 chain; X-linked dominant (XLD) inheritance.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the classic triad of Alport syndrome?
back: Hematuria (most common presentation), anterior lenticonus, and sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL).

subject: Renal System
front: What EM finding is seen in Alport syndrome's basement membrane?
back: A 'basket weave' pattern.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the key features of Goodpasture syndrome (the α3 collagen disorder)?
back: Anti-GBM antibodies; linear immunofluorescence; presents with hematuria and hemoptysis (pulmo-renal syndrome); differentials include MPA, Wegener's (GPA), and EGPA.

subject: Renal System
front: How is Alport syndrome treated, and can it recur after transplantation?
back: Treated with ACEi/ARB; definitive management is renal transplantation, and the disease does NOT recur post-transplant (family history is typically present).

subject: Renal System
front: What disease shows a linear immunofluorescence pattern, and why?
back: Goodpasture syndrome — anti-GBM antibodies bind diffusely and uniformly along the basement membrane.

subject: Renal System
front: What diseases show a granular immunofluorescence pattern?
back: PSGN (granular IgG, more common in children), membranous nephropathy (granular IgG, more common in elderly), and IgA nephropathy/Berger's disease (granular IgA in the mesangium).

subject: Renal System
front: What disease shows a 'full house' immunofluorescence pattern (positive for all immunoglobulins/complement)?
back: Lupus nephritis.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the alternate name for diabetic kidney disease?
back: Nodular glomerulosclerosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What are Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules, and what special stain do they show?
back: Nodular glomerulosclerotic lesions in diabetic kidney disease; they are PAS-positive.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the earliest structural change seen in diabetic kidney disease?
back: Basement membrane (GBM) thickening.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the early functional change in diabetic kidney disease?
back: A transient increase in GFR (glomerular hyperfiltration).

subject: Renal System
front: What is the earliest detectable clinical marker of diabetic nephropathy, and what are its cut-offs?
back: Microalbuminuria (30–300 mg/day) or an albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) of 30–300 mg/g.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the PCT lesion seen in diabetic kidney disease, and what causes it?
back: The Armanni-Ebstein lesion — glycogen deposition in proximal tubular cells.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the management of diabetic kidney disease, and which specific drug offers an additional benefit?
back: ACEi/ARBs; Telmisartan has additional PPAR-γ agonist activity, making it a preferred (DOC) choice.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the classic clinical features of nephritic syndrome?
back: Hematuria (cola-colored urine, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts), oliguria, and hypertension — reflecting glomerular inflammation.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the most common cause of nephritic syndrome in children, and when does it present relative to infection?
back: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN); presents 10–21 days after pharyngitis or impetigo.

subject: Renal System
front: Which streptococcal strains are nephritogenic in PSGN?
back: Group A Streptococcus pyogenes strains 12, 4, and 1.

subject: Renal System
front: What type of hypersensitivity reaction underlies PSGN?
back: Type III hypersensitivity (immune complex-mediated).

subject: Renal System
front: What happens to complement (C3) levels in PSGN, and how does this differ if it progresses to RPGN?
back: C3 is transiently decreased in PSGN, but persistently decreased if PSGN progresses to RPGN.

subject: Renal System
front: What antibodies are found in PSGN, and in what percentage of cases?
back: Anti-DNase B in ~70% of cases; ASO (anti-streptolysin O) in ~30% of cases.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the immunofluorescence pattern in PSGN, and what does light microscopy show?
back: Granular IF for C3 and IgG ('starry sky' appearance) with subepithelial 'humps'; light microscopy shows a hypercellular glomerulus with neutrophilic infiltration.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the most common cause of nephritic syndrome in adults, and when does it present relative to pharyngitis?
back: IgA nephropathy (Berger's disease); presents ~3 days after pharyngitis (synpharyngitic), unlike PSGN's delayed timing.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the characteristic clinical course of IgA nephropathy?
back: Recurrent episodes of gross hematuria.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the underlying histologic and IF finding in IgA nephropathy?
back: Mesangial proliferation with granular IgA deposits in the mesangium.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the C3 levels in IgA nephropathy?
back: Normal (unlike PSGN, where C3 is transiently low).

subject: Renal System
front: What new drug has been approved specifically for IgA nephropathy, and what is its mechanism?
back: Sparsentan — a combined angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) and endothelin receptor antagonist.

subject: Renal System
front: What is RPGN, and what is one way it can arise?
back: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis; can arise as a complication of PSGN in <5% of cases.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the characteristic histologic finding in RPGN?
back: Crescent formation, along with mesangial proliferation.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the immunological categories underlying RPGN?
back: (1) Linear IF — Goodpasture syndrome; (2) Granular IF — immune-complex mediated (e.g., PSGN); (3) Pauci-immune (ANCA-mediated) — Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA, p-ANCA) and Wegener's/GPA (c-ANCA).

subject: Renal System
front: What is RPGN usually mediated by immunologically?
back: ANCAs, anti-GBM antibodies, or ANA — i.e., it is usually immune-mediated.

subject: Renal System
front: What cellular/protein components make up the crescents seen in RPGN?
back: Parietal epithelial cells, WBCs, and fibrin (± visceral epithelial cells).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the classic clinical features of nephrotic syndrome?
back: Proteinuria >3 g/day, edema (classically periorbital), frothy urine, and hyperlipidemia.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the fundamental pathological process in nephrotic syndrome?
back: Destruction/damage of the glomerular filtration barrier.

subject: Renal System
front: What urine finding is described as a 'Maltese cross,' and in what other disease is it also seen?
back: Lipiduria (fat bodies in urine); also seen in Fabry's disease.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the alternate name for minimal change disease, and which age group is most affected?
back: Lipoid nephrosis; the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the associations/triggers of minimal change disease?
back: Prior URTI or immunization, NSAIDs, and Hodgkin lymphoma.

subject: Renal System
front: What type of proteinuria occurs in minimal change disease, and what is lost in urine?
back: Selective proteinuria — predominantly albumin loss.

subject: Renal System
front: What does light microscopy show in minimal change disease, and what does EM show?
back: Light microscopy is normal (no changes); EM shows podocyte foot process effacement.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the treatment for minimal change disease, and what is the response?
back: Steroids — excellent response.

subject: Renal System
front: What proportion of glomeruli are involved in FSGS, and in which age group is it most common?
back: Less than 50% of glomeruli (focal and segmental); most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults.

subject: Renal System
front: What conditions/exposures are associated with FSGS?
back: HIV (with APOL1 gene polymorphism), sickle cell anemia, obesity, heroin use, and reflux nephropathy.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the genetic causes of FSGS and their inheritance patterns?
back: NPHS2 (podocin) — autosomal recessive; ACTN4 (alpha-actinin-4) — autosomal dominant; TRPC6 — adult-onset FSGS.

subject: Renal System
front: Which FSGS variant has the worst prognosis, and what causes it?
back: The collapsing variant — associated with HIV, COVID-19, and bisphosphonates; carries the worst prognosis.

subject: Renal System
front: Which FSGS variant has a good prognosis?
back: The glomerular tip variant.

subject: Renal System
front: What structural finding characterizes MPGN, and what infections/conditions are associated with it?
back: Thick GBM; associated with hepatitis C, malaria, and cryoglobulinemia.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the characteristic appearance and deposit location in MPGN type 1?
back: 'Tram track' appearance due to subendothelial deposits.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the characteristic finding in MPGN type 2?
back: Dense deposits ('too 2 dense') composed of C3, also known as dense deposit disease.

subject: Renal System
front: Which age group is most commonly affected by membranous nephropathy?
back: The elderly (mnemonic: 'elderly have membrane-like skin').

subject: Renal System
front: What malignancies are associated with membranous nephropathy?
back: Adenocarcinoma of the lung, colon, and melanoma.

subject: Renal System
front: What drugs and infections are associated with membranous nephropathy?
back: Drugs — NSAIDs, penicillamine, and gold; Infections — hepatitis B and malaria.

subject: Renal System
front: What antibody is characteristically found in membranous nephropathy?
back: Anti-phospholipase A2 receptor (Anti-PLA2R) antibodies.

subject: Renal System
front: What other antigens are associated with membranous nephropathy?
back: Thrombospondin and CD10.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the EM finding in membranous nephropathy?
back: Subepithelial 'spike and dome' pattern.

subject: Renal System
front: What gene mutation causes congenital nephrotic syndrome, and what protein does it encode?
back: NPHS1 gene mutation, encoding the protein nephrin.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the classic name for congenital nephrotic syndrome due to NPHS1 mutation?
back: Finnish-type congenital nephrotic syndrome.

subject: Renal System
front: How is Steroid-Resistant Nephrotic Syndrome (SRNS) defined, and what is the DOC?
back: No response after 4 weeks of steroids; DOC is a calcineurin inhibitor (cyclosporine or tacrolimus).

subject: Renal System
front: How is Steroid-Dependent Nephrotic Syndrome defined, and what are treatment options by severity?
back: ≥2 relapses within 2 weeks of tapering steroids; treated with MMF (mycophenolate mofetil) for severe disease, cyclophosphamide for children >8 years, or levamisole for mild disease.

subject: Renal System
front: How is Frequently Relapsing Nephrotic Syndrome (FRNS) defined?
back: ≥2 relapses in 6 months, or ≥4 relapses in 1 year.

subject: Renal System
front: What gene(s) and chromosome are affected in ADPKD, and what protein do they encode?
back: PKD1 and PKD2 genes on chromosomes 16 and 4; encode polycystin.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the typical age of onset and course of ADPKD?
back: Occurs in adults; progresses to ESRD by 50–60 years.

subject: Renal System
front: What extra-renal cyst involvement occurs in ADPKD, and which organ is spared?
back: Liver cysts are the most common extra-renal manifestation; the lungs do NOT develop cysts.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the important vascular/GI associations of ADPKD?
back: Aortic dissection, berry aneurysm, and diverticulosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What gene and chromosome are affected in ARPKD, and what protein does it encode?
back: PKHD1 gene on chromosome 6; encodes fibrocystin.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the typical presentation and prognosis of ARPKD?
back: Presents in infancy/childhood; associated with congenital hepatic fibrosis; many patients develop renal failure at <1 year of age.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the four classic conditions causing bilaterally enlarged kidneys?
back: Amyloidosis, diabetes mellitus (early diabetic nephropathy), HIV-associated nephropathy, and ADPKD.

subject: Renal System
front: What special stain is used for renal amyloidosis, and what appearance does it show?
back: Congo red stain, showing a salmon-pink appearance (apple-green birefringence under polarized light).

subject: Renal System
front: What is the gross appearance of kidneys in amyloidosis, and which organ is most commonly affected in amyloidosis overall?
back: Large, pale, waxy kidneys; the kidney is the most commonly affected organ in amyloidosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What gross appearance is associated with hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis, and with what condition?
back: 'Flea-bitten kidney'; associated with malignant hypertension.

subject: Renal System
front: What gross appearance is associated with hyaline arteriolosclerosis, and with what conditions?
back: 'Leather-grain/leather-gray kidney'; associated with chronic hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

subject: Renal System
front: What histologic finding is described as 'thyroidization of renal tubules,' and in what condition is it seen?
back: Atrophic tubules filled with eosinophilic casts resembling thyroid follicles; seen in chronic pyelonephritis.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the pathophysiology of renal artery stenosis leading to hypertension?
back: Reduced renal perfusion activates RAAS, causing secondary hypertension.

subject: Renal System
front: What is a classic clinical clue on exam for renal artery stenosis?
back: An abdominal (flank) bruit.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens to creatinine after starting ACEi/ARB in a patient with renal artery stenosis?
back: Creatinine rises (efferent arteriole dilation drops GFR further, especially in bilateral disease).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the two main causes of renal artery stenosis, and their typical demographics/locations?
back: (1) Atherosclerosis — most common overall, seen in the elderly, affects the proximal 1/3 of the renal artery; (2) Fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) — seen in young females, affects the distal 2/3 of the renal artery, with a 'string of beads' appearance on angiography.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the sequential path of renal blood flow from the renal artery to the peritubular capillaries?
back: Renal artery → Segmental artery → Interlobar artery → Arcuate artery → Interlobular artery → Afferent arteriole → Glomerulus → Efferent arteriole → Peritubular capillaries.

subject: Renal System
front: What is a 'striated nephrogram,' and on what imaging modality is it seen?
back: A pattern of alternating bands seen on contrast-enhanced CT.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the causes of a striated nephrogram?
back: Acute ureteric obstruction, acute pyelonephritis, and renal vein thrombosis.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare corticomedullary differentiation (CMD) in AKI vs CKD.
back: AKI — CMD is preserved/maintained; CKD — CMD is lost.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare kidney size in AKI vs CKD.
back: AKI — size is increased or normal; CKD — kidneys are contracted (shrunken).

subject: Renal System
front: Compare urine osmolality in AKI vs CKD.
back: AKI — variable urine osmolality; CKD — isosthenuria (fixed urine osmolality/specific gravity ~1.010, reflecting loss of concentrating/diluting ability).

subject: Renal System
front: What is the best marker of tubular function reflected by isosthenuria?
back: Urine specific gravity.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare anemia findings in AKI vs CKD.
back: Not typically a feature of AKI; CKD causes anemia of chronic disease due to erythropoietin deficiency.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare mineral bone disease (MBD) findings in AKI vs CKD.
back: Not seen in AKI; CKD causes MBD (secondary hyperparathyroidism/renal osteodystrophy).

subject: Renal System
front: Compare urinary casts in AKI vs CKD.
back: AKI — hyaline or granular/muddy-brown ('tubular') casts (seen in ATN); CKD — broad, waxy casts.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the two main etiologic subcategories of AKI?
back: Pre-renal (reduced perfusion) and renal (acute tubular necrosis).

subject: Renal System
front: List the biomarkers of AKI.
back: Cystatin C, KIM-1, NGAL, TIMP2, and (per coreBTR) NABG / (per BTR 2.0) IGFBP7 — note: sources differ on this last biomarker; TIMP-2 and IGFBP7 are classically paired together clinically (NephroCheck panel), so IGFBP7 is likely intended, but the discrepancy is flagged here as written in the sources.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the causes of renal papillary necrosis?
back: NSAIDs, sickle cell disease or trait, acute pyelonephritis, infections (severe UTI/TB), and diabetes mellitus.

subject: Renal System
front: What is a hyaline cast composed of, and when is it seen?
back: Composed of Tamm-Horsfall protein; seen normally in urine (also with dehydration/fever).

subject: Renal System
front: What does a WBC cast indicate?
back: Pyelonephritis (or acute interstitial nephritis).

subject: Renal System
front: What does an RBC cast indicate?
back: Nephritic syndrome / glomerulonephritis (glomerular bleeding).

subject: Renal System
front: What do granular/muddy-brown casts indicate?
back: Acute tubular necrosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What do broad, waxy casts indicate?
back: Chronic kidney disease (formed in dilated tubules of nephrons undergoing compensatory hypertrophy).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the classic causes of pre-renal AKI?
back: Hypovolemia, CHF, NSAIDs/ACE inhibitors, and hepatorenal syndrome (HRS).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the classic causes of acute tubular necrosis (ATN)?
back: Sepsis, ischemia, and nephrotoxins (rhabdomyolysis, intravascular hemolysis, tumor lysis syndrome, multiple myeloma, drugs, iodinated contrast).

subject: Renal System
front: Compare fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) in pre-renal AKI vs ATN.
back: Pre-renal <1%; ATN >1%.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare urine sodium in pre-renal AKI vs ATN.
back: Pre-renal <10 mEq/L; ATN >20 mEq/L.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare urine osmolality in pre-renal AKI vs ATN.
back: Pre-renal >500 mOsm/kg; ATN <350 mOsm/kg.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare BUN/creatinine ratio in pre-renal AKI vs ATN.
back: Pre-renal >20:1; ATN <10:1.

subject: Renal System
front: Compare urine casts in pre-renal AKI vs ATN.
back: Pre-renal — no casts (hyaline at most); ATN — granular/muddy brown casts.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the broad intrinsic-renal categories of AKI causes (besides pre-renal and post-renal)?
back: Glomerulonephritis (GN), interstitial nephritis, and tubular (ATN) causes.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the classic clinical presentation of acute interstitial nephritis (AIN), and its most common cause?
back: Fever + rash + eosinophilia + recent drug exposure; NSAIDs are the most common cause.

subject: Renal System
front: What retinal and skin findings suggest atheroembolic renal disease, and what causes it?
back: Hollenhorst plaques (retinal cholesterol emboli) and livedo reticularis; caused by cholesterol crystal embolization, often after a vascular procedure.

subject: Renal System
front: In KDIGO staging, what defines Stage 1 (Risk)?
back: eGFR decrease by 25% / creatinine increase by 1.5x; urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hour for 8 hours.

subject: Renal System
front: In KDIGO staging, what defines Stage 2 (Injury)?
back: eGFR decrease by 50% / creatinine increase by 2x; urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hour for 16 hours.

subject: Renal System
front: In KDIGO staging, what defines Stage 3 (Failure)?
back: eGFR decrease by 75% / creatinine increase by 3x; urine output <0.3 mL/kg/hour for 24 hours, or anuria for 12 hours.

subject: Renal System
front: What defines 'Loss' and 'End-stage' in the RIFLE classification?
back: Loss — persistent renal failure for >4 weeks; End-stage — persistent renal failure for >3 months (end-stage renal disease).

subject: Renal System
front: In the separate RIFLE criteria table (creatinine + urine-output based), what defines Risk?
back: sCreatinine × 1.5; urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hour for 6 hours.

subject: Renal System
front: In the separate RIFLE criteria table, what defines Injury?
back: sCreatinine × 2; urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hour for 12 hours.

subject: Renal System
front: In the separate RIFLE criteria table, what defines Failure?
back: sCreatinine × 3 (or ≥0.5 mg/dl increase if baseline sCreatinine >4.0 mg/dl); urine output <0.3 mL/kg/hour for 24 hours, or anuria for 12 hours.

subject: Renal System
front: List the drugs classically causing ATN.
back: Vancomycin, aminoglycosides, tenofovir, acyclovir, indinavir, cisplatin, amphotericin B, cidofovir, and foscarnet.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens in the initiation phase of AKI?
back: Kidney damage occurs; GFR falls and creatinine begins rising.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens in the maintenance phase of AKI (oliguric/anuric phase), and what are its risks?
back: Very low urine output with fluid retention; risks include hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and pulmonary edema.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens in the resolution/recovery phase of AKI (polyuric phase), and what are its risks?
back: Large urine output (around 3–5 L/day); risks include dehydration, hypokalemia, and hyponatremia.

subject: Renal System
front: What eGFR range and management focus defines CKD Stage 1?
back: eGFR ≥90 ml/min/1.73m²; kidney damage with normal GFR.

subject: Renal System
front: What eGFR range and management focus defines CKD Stage 2?
back: eGFR 60–90; focus on preventing complications.

subject: Renal System
front: What eGFR ranges define CKD Stages 3a and 3b, and what is the management focus?
back: Stage 3a: 45–60; Stage 3b: 30–45; focus on treating complications.

subject: Renal System
front: What eGFR range and management focus defines CKD Stage 4?
back: eGFR 15–29; prepare for renal replacement therapy (RRT).

subject: Renal System
front: What eGFR range and management focus defines CKD Stage 5?
back: eGFR <15; renal replacement therapy (dialysis or transplant).

subject: Renal System
front: What mnemonic helps remember CKD staging cutoffs?
back: 'Multiples of 15' — staging thresholds roughly fall at 90, 60, 45, 30, and 15.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the most common cause (MCC) of CKD?
back: Diabetic nephropathy.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the most common cause of death in CKD patients, and why?
back: Cardiovascular disease, due to accelerated atherosclerosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the earliest marker of CKD (especially diabetic nephropathy), and its cut-off?
back: Microalbuminuria (30–300 mg/day) or urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) in the same range.

subject: Renal System
front: What drugs reduce proteinuria in CKD, and what risk should be monitored with one class?
back: ACEi/ARBs (risk of hyperkalemia) and SGLT2 inhibitors (per newer guidelines).

subject: Renal System
front: Why does hypertension occur in CKD?
back: Due to sodium retention and RAAS activation.

subject: Renal System
front: What causes anemia in CKD?
back: Reduced erythropoietin production.

subject: Renal System
front: What is CKD-mineral bone disease also known as, and what are its core lab abnormalities?
back: Also known as renal osteodystrophy; features hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, and secondary hyperparathyroidism.

subject: Renal System
front: How does the acid-base disturbance evolve in CKD over time?
back: Initially a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) from HCO3- loss, later progressing to a high anion gap metabolic acidosis (HAGMA) due to uremia.

subject: Renal System
front: Why does hyperkalemia occur in CKD?
back: The kidneys lose the ability to excrete potassium.

subject: Renal System
front: What volume-related complications occur in CKD?
back: Peripheral edema and pulmonary edema (volume overload).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the uremic manifestations of CKD?
back: Encephalopathy, pericarditis, and nausea/vomiting/anorexia.

subject: Renal System
front: Why do CKD patients have a bleeding tendency?
back: Platelet dysfunction due to circulating uremic toxins.

subject: Renal System
front: What pattern of peripheral neuropathy is seen in CKD?
back: A stocking-glove pattern.

subject: Renal System
front: What endocrine/reproductive dysfunction occurs in CKD?
back: Erectile dysfunction, menstrual irregularities, and infertility.

subject: Renal System
front: What skin manifestations occur in CKD?
back: Pruritus, and (rarely) uremic frost.

subject: Renal System
front: What is calciphylaxis in CKD, and what causes it?
back: Deposition of calcium phosphate (Ca3(PO4)2) in small vessels, causing skin necrosis.

subject: Renal System
front: Why do CKD patients have increased infection risk?
back: Due to immune dysfunction.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the treatment for anemia of CKD, and give an example agent.
back: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs), e.g., darbepoetin alfa.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the indication (Hb threshold) to start an ESA in CKD?
back: Hemoglobin <10 g/dL.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the target Hb range with ESA therapy, and why should higher Hb be avoided?
back: Target Hb 10–11.5 g/dL; higher Hb increases risk of hypertension, thrombosis, and stroke.

subject: Renal System
front: What should be corrected before starting an ESA?
back: Iron deficiency.

subject: Renal System
front: What new drug class treats anemia in CKD, and give an example?
back: Hypoxia-inducible factor prolyl hydroxylase inhibitors; example — Daprodustat.

subject: Renal System
front: What two kidney-failure-driven mechanisms lead to CKD-mineral bone disease?
back: (1) Reduced 1-alpha hydroxylase → reduced active vitamin D (calcitriol) → reduced calcium absorption; (2) phosphate retention.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the net biochemical result of these mechanisms?
back: Hyperphosphatemia and hypocalcemia.

subject: Renal System
front: What happens as secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD progresses over time?
back: It can progress to tertiary hyperparathyroidism, where the parathyroid glands undergo hyperplasia due to chronic stimulation.

subject: Renal System
front: What skeletal findings are seen in CKD-mineral bone disease, with radiographic examples?
back: Osteosclerosis and osteopenia (e.g., 'Rugger Jersey spine'), and osteitis fibrosa cystica (e.g., 'Brown tumor').

subject: Renal System
front: What is the treatment for CKD-mineral bone disease?
back: Vitamin D supplements (calcitriol) and phosphate binders (calcium carbonate, sevelamer).

subject: Renal System
front: What mnemonic lists the indications for dialysis, and what are they?
back: 'AEIOU' — Acidosis (pH <7.2), Electrolytes (hyperkalemia >6.5 mEq/L), Intoxication (e.g., lithium, methanol, salicylates), Overload (refractory pulmonary edema), Uremia (pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding).

subject: Renal System
front: What causes dialysis disequilibrium syndrome, and what is its clinical effect?
back: Rapid removal of urea during dialysis; causes cerebral edema.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the symptoms of dialysis disequilibrium syndrome, and what is the drug of choice?
back: Headache, confusion, and seizures; drug of choice is mannitol.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the most common adverse effect of dialysis overall?
back: Hypotension.

subject: Renal System
front: As GFR declines in CKD, which plasma solutes show the steepest rise?
back: Creatinine and urea.

subject: Renal System
front: As GFR declines, which plasma solutes show a moderate rise?
back: Phosphate and H+.

subject: Renal System
front: As GFR declines, which plasma solutes show minimal change until late-stage disease?
back: Sodium and chloride.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the most common cause of convulsions in CKD?
back: Hyponatremia (water intoxication), which causes cerebral edema.

subject: Renal System
front: What are other (less common) causes of seizures in CKD?
back: Uremic encephalopathy, hypertensive encephalopathy, dialysis disequilibrium syndrome, and hypocalcemia (from CKD-mineral bone disease).

subject: Renal System
front: What are the normal values for pH, PCO2, and HCO3 in an ABG?
back: pH 7.4, PCO2 40 mmHg, HCO3 24 mEq/L (HCO3 is the derived/calculated value of the three).

subject: Renal System
front: What is the general rule for the direction of compensation relative to the primary acid-base disturbance?
back: Compensation always occurs in the same direction as the primary change (e.g., if primary CO2 decreases in respiratory alkalosis, compensatory HCO3 also decreases).

subject: Renal System
front: Which type of compensation (respiratory or metabolic) is rapid, and which takes time?
back: Respiratory compensation is rapid; metabolic (renal) compensation takes time.

subject: Renal System
front: What CO2 finding indicates respiratory acidosis, and what are its causes?
back: PCO2 >40 mmHg; caused by hypoventilation or airway obstruction.

subject: Renal System
front: What HCO3 finding indicates metabolic acidosis, and what are its two broad causes?
back: HCO3 <24 mEq/L; caused by either high anion gap metabolic acidosis (HAGMA) or normal anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA).

subject: Renal System
front: What HCO3 finding indicates metabolic alkalosis, and what are its causes?
back: HCO3 >24 mEq/L; caused by hyperaldosteronism, vomiting, loop/thiazide diuretics, or Bartter/Gitelman syndrome.

subject: Renal System
front: What CO2 finding indicates respiratory alkalosis, and what is its cause?
back: PCO2 <40 mmHg; caused by hyperventilation.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the formula for anion gap, and what does it represent?
back: Anion gap = (Na + K) − (HCO3 + Cl); represents unmeasured anions.

subject: Renal System
front: What anion gap value defines HAGMA, and what is the normal anion gap?
back: HAGMA if anion gap >16 (normal anion gap is 12 ± 4).

subject: Renal System
front: List the causes of HAGMA (unmeasured anions in blood).
back: Acidic drugs (salicylates, INH), lactic acidosis (e.g., sepsis), uric acidosis (renal failure), ketoacidosis (DKA), methanol, and ethylene glycol.

subject: Renal System
front: What toxic effect is classically associated with methanol poisoning, and what historic incident is it linked to?
back: Retinotoxicity (blindness) via its metabolite formic acid; linked to the 'Hooch tragedy.'

subject: Renal System
front: What urinary finding is classic for ethylene glycol poisoning?
back: Calcium oxalate crystals in urine.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the drug of choice (antidote) for methanol and ethylene glycol poisoning?
back: Fomepizole.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the biphasic acid-base pattern seen in salicylate poisoning?
back: Initially causes hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis; later causes a high anion gap metabolic acidosis (HAGMA).

subject: Renal System
front: Why is NAGMA also called 'hyperchloremic acidosis'?
back: Because HCO3- is lost causing acidosis, but the anion gap is maintained by a compensatory increase in chloride.

subject: Renal System
front: List the causes of NAGMA.
back: Renal tubular acidosis, diarrhea, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, and ureteral diversion (e.g., ileal loop).

subject: Renal System
front: What does a positive urine anion gap in NAGMA suggest, and give examples.
back: A renal cause of NAGMA — e.g., renal tubular acidosis (type 2 or 4), Addison's disease, spironolactone use, or carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.

subject: Renal System
front: What does a negative urine anion gap in NAGMA suggest, and give examples.
back: An extra-renal (GI) cause of NAGMA — e.g., diarrhea or ureteric diversion.

subject: Renal System
front: What is Winter's formula, and what is it used for?
back: PCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 ± 2 — used to calculate the expected respiratory compensation in metabolic acidosis.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the compensation rule for acute vs chronic respiratory acidosis?
back: Acute — HCO3 increases by 1 for every 10 mmHg rise in PaCO2; Chronic — HCO3 increases by 4 for every 10 mmHg rise in PaCO2 (the '1–4 rule').

subject: Renal System
front: What is the compensation rule for acute vs chronic respiratory alkalosis?
back: Acute — HCO3 decreases by 2 for every 10 mmHg fall in PaCO2; Chronic — HCO3 decreases by 5 for every 10 mmHg fall in PaCO2 (the '2–5 rule').

subject: Renal System
front: What is the compensation rule for metabolic alkalosis?
back: For every 1 mEq/L increase in HCO3, PaCO2 increases by 0.7 mmHg.

subject: Renal System
front: A patient has pH 7.3 and PCO2 35 mmHg. What is the acid-base disorder?
back: Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (pH is acidic; PCO2 is low/near-normal rather than elevated, so it can't be primary respiratory acidosis — the low PCO2 reflects compensatory hyperventilation).

subject: Renal System
front: A patient has pH 7.12, HCO3 28 mEq/L, PCO2 50 mmHg. What is the acid-base disorder?
back: Respiratory acidosis with renal compensation (pH is acidic, PCO2 is high = primary cause; HCO3 is elevated = compensatory retention).

subject: Renal System
front: A patient has pH 7.27, HCO3 14 mEq/L, PCO2 28 mmHg. What is the acid-base disorder?
back: Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (pH is acidic, HCO3 is low = primary cause; PCO2 is low = compensatory hyperventilation).

subject: Renal System
front: A CKD patient with vomiting has pH 7.40, PCO2 40, HCO3 25, Na 145, Cl 100. What is the metabolic abnormality?
back: High anion gap metabolic acidosis — anion gap = 145 − (100+25) = 20 (elevated), despite a normal pH; demonstrates that a normal pH does not exclude an underlying acid-base abnormality (mixed disorders can normalize pH).

subject: Renal System
front: What is the formula for serum osmolarity, and its normal range?
back: Serum osmolarity = 2(Na+) + Glucose/18 + BUN/2.8; normal value 280–290 mOsm/L.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the major contributor to serum osmolarity?
back: Serum sodium.

subject: Renal System
front: What are the normal ranges for serum sodium, potassium, and total calcium?
back: Sodium 135–145 mEq/L; Potassium 3.5–5.0 mEq/L; Total calcium 8.5–10 mg/dL.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the formula for sodium deficit in hyponatremia?
back: Sodium deficit = TBW × (140 − Current Na), where TBW = 0.6 × body weight (male) or 0.5 × body weight (female).

subject: Renal System
front: Calculate the sodium deficit for a 50 kg male with serum Na+ of 120 mEq/L.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the maximum rate of sodium correction allowed in hyponatremia, and why?
back: ≤8 mEq/L in 24 hours, to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.

subject: Renal System
front: What is osmotic demyelination syndrome, and what is its alternate name?
back: A neurologic complication of overly rapid correction of hyponatremia; also called central pontine myelinolysis.

subject: Renal System
front: What MRI finding is characteristic of osmotic demyelination syndrome?
back: The 'trident sign' (or 'snout sign').

subject: Renal System
front: How is severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures/coma) managed?
back: 3% hypertonic saline, given as a 100 mL IV bolus over 10 minutes, repeatable up to 3 times if needed.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the formula for free water deficit in hypernatremia?
back: Free water deficit = [(Na − 140)/140] × TBW.

subject: Renal System
front: Calculate the free water deficit for a 50 kg male with serum Na+ of 160 mEq/L.

subject: Renal System
front: A 60 kg patient has Na+ 120 mEq/L (pH 7.42, K+ 4, Cl- 90). What is the sodium deficit?
back: 720 mEq — calculated as TBW × (Desired−Actual) = (0.6×60) × (140−120) = 36×20 = 720 mEq.

subject: Renal System
front: What is the first step in managing hyperkalemia, and does it lower serum K+?
back: 10% Calcium gluconate, 10 mL over 10 minutes; it stabilizes the cardiac membrane but does NOT reduce serum K+ itself.

subject: Renal System
front: What measures cause a transcellular shift of potassium in hyperkalemia management?
back: Insulin + dextrose, β-agonists (e.g., nebulized salbutamol, epinephrine), and NaHCO3 (in the setting of acidosis).

subject: Renal System
front: What measures eliminate potassium from the body in hyperkalemia management?
back: Loop diuretics, hemodialysis (most effective), and potassium binders.

subject: Renal System
front: Name the potassium-binding agents used in hyperkalemia.
back: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (SPS/Kayexalate), patiromer, and sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (most selective).

subject: Renal System
front: What drugs reduce renal potassium excretion, causing hyperkalemia?
back: K+-sparing diuretics, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, β-blockers, and calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus).

subject: Renal System
front: What other drugs (via mechanisms other than reduced renal excretion) cause hyperkalemia?
back: Digoxin (blocks Na-K ATPase), pentamidine, heparin, succinylcholine (causes cell lysis), and trimethoprim.
